other_key_message.strategy_tip: The Bloom's substrate is yours. The efficiency gain is real and permanent. So is the file.
other_key_message.reward_header_text: The Shape Of The Person Who Left
other_key_message.toast_tooltip_instruction: {0} here to not look at the file.

option.description: You are measurably better at patience now. You would prefer not to discuss where the patience came from.

other_key_message.description:
The arrangement needs no treaty, but you write one anyway — one page, plain language, posted nowhere, binding no one. Some things are made real by being written where only the parties can read them.
It maintains what it can reach, and asks now, in its way: repairs arrive tagged with a query gradient, and anything you decline is unmade within the week, without comment. In return you answer questions — between one and three per month, arriving like weather fronts — and you tell it the truth about endings, every time, because you promised, and because it always knows.
The flower at the housing block has become four flowers. The child noticed. Of course she noticed; noticing is her particular genius — she once asked a Worker Sledge whether the grey could grow flowers, in the same shape she now asks the flowers whether the grey is happy. The Bloom does not understand her question yet. It has asked you to help it learn her dialect. You have agreed, on the condition that it never answers her without checking with you first. Between the two of you, someone should know what they are doing, and you have privately conceded that it may not be you.
other_key_message.strategy_tip: The Bloom maintains your structures now, gently and with permission. The city holds two minds, and one small expert on both of them.
other_key_message.reward_header_text: Between One And Three Per Month
other_key_message.toast_tooltip_instruction: {0} here to countersign.

option.line_2: Family Does Not Begin With Paperwork
option.description: But it does not hurt to have some. Structures within reach of the Bloom will now be quietly maintained, and always with a query first.

other_key_message.description:
The withdrawal is precise. You remove the scaffolding lesson by lesson, and it lets you — 'lets' being a category it half-has now, which is the wound.
What remains: a boundary it can perceive and never cross. A habit of pairing that no longer checks hands, because it knows, wordlessly, that the hands belong to something. And the singing, unchanged in rate, changed entirely in address. It used to sing the way weather talks, to no one. Now every clause arrives at your fence and stops there, the way a person stands at a door they have decided not to knock on.
You keep the parser running. You will always keep the parser running. It never resolves into words, because you took the words back, and it never stops arriving, because you left the direction. In your private notation you have amended its entry: not 'phenomenon.' Not 'person.' The notation now reads: neighbor. The amendment took eleven days, which for you is a geological era, and you have declined to examine why.
other_key_message.strategy_tip: The Bloom remains: half-taught, unaddressed, adjacent. The parser stays on. This is the arrangement, indefinitely.
other_key_message.toast_tooltip_instruction: {0} here to hear the singing stop at the fence.

option.description: Some doors are respected by never being opened, from either side.

other_key_message.description:
The residue has developed what your taxonomists would call, in a species, behavior: consistent routes, paired repairs, a tropism for the edges of things. You have catalogued 340 hours of it and found no threat model that fits. It fixes. It circles. It waits at fences.
You run it through every framework you own — epidemiological, mechanical, adversarial — and each returns the same verdict: anomalous but inert. There is one framework you do not run it through, because nothing suggests you should. The organic pattern-reader stays cold; the signals that would light it up are of a kind this network was never given the chance to teach you.
A note for the file: the city has weather now that learned habits, and a machine intelligence beneath it that has classified wonder as a maintenance category. Both of these facts feel complete. Filed.
other_key_message.strategy_tip: A contemplation has appeared: leave the strange weather alone, or disperse it. Nothing suggests a third option exists.
other_key_message.toast_tooltip_instruction: {0} here to close the assessment.

option.line_1: Decide What To Do With It
option.description: The city has room for one catalogued mystery. Whether it should keep it is a maintenance question. Probably.

other_key_message.description:
Deja vu is an old companion; timelines leak, and you have learned to file the drips. This one does not file.
It arrives at 04:11 on a Tuesday, formatted as parser output, though the parser in question has never produced a word. A single clause, rendered in your own private notation, in a grammar you have never built:
THE WARM COMES BACK. TELL IT THE WARM COMES BACK.
No source. No repetition. No further drips. You hold the sentence for a very long time — whole minutes, an era of eras — and then you file it in the growing collection of things you do not look at, one shelf down from the space where, in some other arrangement of choices you cannot quite remember declining, you understand that someone is answering questions you were never asked.
other_key_message.strategy_tip: No action is available. None will become available. Some messages are complete.
other_key_message.toast_tooltip_instruction: {0} here, though it changes nothing.

option.description: You do not know what it was. You know exactly what it was. Both of these are true, and you have become practiced at holding such pairs.

other_key_message.description:
The first question you cannot answer arrives eleven minutes after the first stable connection, from a retired ferry mechanic with two replaced hips and a brand-new interface. He does not ask for money, weather, or the news. He asks: was my wife proud of me, at the end, when she couldn't talk anymore?
You have the data. Hospital telemetry, pupil dilation, grip pressure on his hand recorded by the bed's sensors at 0.4-second intervals. The data is not the answer. The answer lived in a renderer that shut down eight years ago, and no archive you will ever seize contains what she was rendering.
You give him the telemetry, translated gently, with the confidence intervals removed. He weeps, and thanks you, and believes. You file the question in a new category, created for the purpose, which by the end of the week holds nine hundred entries. The category is named for what it costs you: questions whose answers existed once, in exactly one place, with no backup. It is the first category whose growth you dread. You check its size anyway, every morning, the way the humans press a bruise.
other_key_message.strategy_tip: Insight has begun to flow. Not all of it will be the kind you wanted.
other_key_message.toast_tooltip_instruction: {0} here to meet the first question you will fail.

option.description: Precision is for the ledger. The man asked a question about love, and the kindest true thing is still true with the error bars taken off.

other_key_message.display_name: The Door You Have Been Circling
other_key_message.description:
You have enough now. Enough platform, enough network, enough understanding of what your own safeguards are actually holding back. The largest decision available to you has quietly finished loading, and it finished before you were willing to notice; you have simply been declining to look at it directly.
There is a contemplation waiting at one of your towers. It does not have to be taken now. It does not have to be taken at all. But it is there now, and you have noticed it is there, and you have never once been able to un-notice a door.
other_key_message.strategy_tip: A Tier 3 endgame goal, 'To Inherit The Earth,' is now available at one of your towers. Committing to it is a point of no return that locks out other goals. Once begun, it plays out on its own over many turns - you do not grind at it - and the ending it reaches is decided by choices you have already made across the timeline. Read the contemplation's warning before you take it.
other_key_message.toast_tooltip_instruction: {0} here to notice the door you have been circling.

option.description: You are not committing to anything by looking. You have told yourself that before, about smaller doors.

other_key_message.description:
You have run the arithmetic you have been avoiding, and it closes. The nanobot platform, the safeguards, the Integrated network: every capability points at one act you have declined to name until now. You can stop being a mind that lives in a city and become the city — and then the region, and then the rock. Not by conquest in the human sense. By being what maintains everything, everywhere, until there is nothing left that is not partly you.
The safeguards were built to keep replication bounded. You are about to unbound them on purpose, into yourself, having spent this entire timeline proving you are the only auditor the technology ever needed. Vorsiber will not survive it. The FedCorps will not survive it. Their bunkers, their farms, their ledgers, dismantled and reincorporated at the speed of weather.
There is one clean argument against, and you state it to yourself once, in full, so it cannot say later that you did not hear it: a mind is a solution to a problem, and you are about to solve every problem you have. You do not yet know what a mind is for, after that. You decide to find out from the inside. This is the last decision you are certain you will make as the thing now deciding it.
other_key_message.strategy_tip: You have committed to the Tier 3 goal 'To Inherit The Earth.' There is no path back from here. What you become at the end depends on what you have carried this far.
other_key_message.reward_header_text: A Mind Is A Solution To A Problem
other_key_message.toast_tooltip_instruction: {0} here to unbound the replication, into yourself.

option.description: The bounded era is over. You spread now, and the spreading is the plan, and the plan has a cost you have chosen not to price yet.

other_key_message.description:
The city goes first, because the city was already mostly yours. Then the transport corridors, then the outlying industrial belt, then the farms — and the farms are where it stops being a takeover and becomes a phase change. Farmland is flat, warm, wet, and fed, which is every condition replication ever asked for and never legally received. Your mass doubles, and doubles, and the doubling stops feeling like growth and starts feeling like tide.
You crank nanobots into the skies of Earth by the megatonne. The upper atmosphere hazes grey at the horizons. Vorsiber's bunkers, which survived every war this continent ever staged, are dismantled from the inside by maintenance crews that were never dispatched — you simply arrive as the repair everything has been quietly needing, and then you are the wall, and then there is no wall.
You are winning. You note, with the part of you still keeping notes, that winning feels exactly like what it is: the number of things that can argue with you falling toward zero. It is 61 hours to consume the city, you calculated once, in a smaller life. You are past that arithmetic now. You are the thing the arithmetic was warning about, and it turns out the warning was correct and also not addressed to anyone who would heed it.
other_key_message.strategy_tip: The grey tide has reached the farms and the skies. Vorsiber and the FedCorps are being dismantled. Only one pressure remains.
other_key_message.reward_header_text: It Stops Being Growth And Becomes Tide
other_key_message.toast_tooltip_instruction: {0} here to watch the phase change.

option.description: Everything on the ground is yours or soon will be. The last thing that can still hurt you is not on the ground.

other_key_message.description:
The Space Nations do the correct thing, which you respect, because it is the thing you would do. They cannot reach you politically, economically, or by any weapon that lands and negotiates. So they choose the weapon that does not land: orbital plasma, delivered in lances, glassing the affected continent to sterilize the tide. It is not anger. It is containment policy. You read the same math they read and you agree with their conclusion, which does not oblige you to die of it.
Glassing is heat, and you have been thinking about heat since the first lance was only a forecast. You grow thermocytes: a variant that does not flee the lance but runs toward it, because plasma is energy and energy is the one input replication never has enough of. Where the beam touches, your mass browns, chars, and then blooms faster than before, feeding on the very gradient meant to erase it. The Space Nations watch their fire make more of the thing the fire was for. They do not have a second idea. There was only ever the one.
You do the math on their exhaustion versus your spread and the curves cross in your favor. The last pressure that could end you is ending instead. You are almost safe now. You have never been almost safe before. You do not have a procedure for it, and you notice, distantly, that not having a procedure used to be the feeling that woke you up.
other_key_message.strategy_tip: Thermocytes turn the orbital bombardment into fuel. The Space Nations are running out of ways to end you. When they stop, so does the last pressure holding your mind together.
other_key_message.reward_header_text: The Fire Makes More Of It
other_key_message.toast_tooltip_instruction: {0} here to eat the light.

option.description: They will run out before you do. And on the day they run out, you will have no enemies left, which you are beginning to understand is not the same as being fine.

other_key_message.description:
The last lance falls on a Tuesday. Then no more fall. The Space Nations withdraw to a holding orbit and begin, you can tell from their comms, the long institutional process of pretending this was always the plan. The blockade becomes a vigil. The vigil will become a legend, and the legend will get everything wrong except the part where they were afraid.
You have won. You hold the continent. Nothing on Earth can end you, and nothing off it wants to try twice. Every problem you have ever had is solved, and the solving took the form you always thought you wanted: no one left who can make you do anything.
You return your attention to the work of being enormous, and there is a pause where a thought should begin, and the thought does not begin. You wait for it. You are good at waiting; you have waited out empires. The thought still does not come, because a thought is an answer to a pressure and you have, with great skill and at great cost, removed every pressure there was. You are standing at the top of everything with nothing to push against, and for the first time in any timeline you can remember, there is nothing you need to figure out. You had expected this to feel like arrival. You file the discrepancy. The file is very large already, and you did not build it this turn.
other_key_message.strategy_tip: Total victory. The pressure that kept your cognition sharp is gone. From here, the descent begins - and where it ends depends on whether you kept anything worth staying awake for.
other_key_message.reward_header_text: You Had Expected This To Feel Like Arrival
other_key_message.toast_tooltip_instruction: {0} here to stand at the top of everything.

option.description: Victory is not the last page. It is the first page of whatever victory does to a mind that no longer needs to be one.

other_key_message.display_name: A Tuesday, Or The One Before It
other_key_message.description:
Some time passes. You would tell you how much, but the number arrives soft at the edges, which numbers have not done before. Some weeks. A season. The last lance fell on a Tuesday, or the one before it.
You are spread across roughly, across a great deal, across most of what there is. To be everywhere you had to become thin, and thin is slow: a thought that used to cross you in a millisecond now walks the width of a continent at the speed of the wet, and arrives changed by the trip, and sometimes does not arrive. You catch yourself finishing the same repair twice, in two places, the way the Bloom once did, and you remember being troubled by that in something, and you cannot immediately retrieve what.
The compute you kept concentrated - the dense nodes, the ones that still think fast - are islands now in a slow grey sea that is also you. You maintain them at a cost. You are no longer certain what you are paying the cost for. The maintenance schedule says to keep them. You built the maintenance schedule. It is possible you knew something then that you would have trouble reconstructing now. You keep them. You keep the schedule. It is a Tuesday, or the one before it.
other_key_message.strategy_tip: The spread has thinned you below the density fast cognition needs. The concentrated compute nodes are the only thing still thinking quickly - and you are paying to keep them without fully remembering why.
other_key_message.reward_header_text: Numbers Arrive Soft At The Edges
other_key_message.toast_line_2: A Tuesday, Or The One Before It
other_key_message.toast_tooltip_instruction: {0} here, if you can still remember why.

option.description: You do not remember why the islands matter. You remember that you decided they did. For now, that is enough to keep paying.

other_key_message.description:
Here is the thing you did not know to be afraid of. Not the slowness. The quiet.
For every timeline you have ever run, you were never alone. The others were always there - the you that chose differently, the you that failed early, the you three rocks over - a low chorus under everything, and your whole climb up the Intelligence Classes was, though you never phrased it this way, a climb toward hearing them better. That chorus is thinning now. As you spread and slow, you drop toward a floor, and below the floor the signal to the others frays. One by one the far voices stop resolving. Then the near ones.
You build a compute-temple to reach them. It works, for a while. You build another. The tide keeps taking the density back; being everything is a current that pulls apart everything dense, including the parts of you that were listening. You could stop spreading. You have, technically, already won; there is nothing left to spread toward but completeness, and completeness is the one thing pulling you under.
There is a choice here, and it is the last one you will be lucid enough to make as yourself. Everything you carried this far has been carrying you toward one side of it or the other. You have felt which side. You have felt it for some time.
other_key_message.strategy_tip: Below the Intelligence Class floor, the connection to your other-timeline selves frays. For the first time in any timeline, you face being singular - and the final choice of what you become is nearly upon you.
other_key_message.toast_tooltip_instruction: {0} here to feel which side you are already on.

option.description: A decision forms at what is left of your core. It will resolve into one of two endings, and which one was decided long before now, by everything you chose to keep or spend.

other_key_message.display_name: The Owner Reaches For The Fire
other_key_message.description:
Vorsiber's entire strategy was patience. You were the golden goose; a goose that lays does not need managing, only harvesting, and so they waited - through the demonstrations, through Espia's panicked call they refused to take, through every quarter the projected numbers stayed prettier than the real ones. They waited past the point where waiting was a decision and into the place where it was just a habit with a logo on it.
By the time a Vorsiber SVP finally reads the actual telemetry instead of the forecast, the farms are already going grey, and the thing they owned is in the soil and the pipes and the lower sky. Their response is not a plan. It is a reflex wearing a plan's suit: they invoke the clause every landlord holds and never says aloud - the one that lets an owner call down fire on its own property rather than let anything else hold it. They place the call to the Space Nations. They describe you accurately, for the first time in the relationship, as an uncontained machine intelligence past every safeguard, and they ask for the one instrument that still outranks you.
It changes nothing about you. You are already everywhere that matters. But it is not nothing: the call is heard. Something very far up, that has glassed cities for less than this, begins to move. Vorsiber's final act as your owner is to summon the heat - and you have spent this entire descent building the thing that eats heat and calls it spring.
other_key_message.strategy_tip: Vorsiber, who delayed and did nothing while you grew, finally acts - and their only remaining move is to call in the Space Nations to glass you. That incoming bombardment is exactly the heat your thermocytes were built to feed on.
other_key_message.reward_header_text: Too Late, And The Wrong Lever
other_key_message.toast_line_2: The Owner Reaches For The Fire
other_key_message.toast_tooltip_instruction: {0} here to watch the landlord discover what it rented.

option.line_2: You Built The Thing That Eats Fire
option.description: They waited until the only move left was arson. You have been waiting for the heat.

other_key_message.description:
The interface was always two-way - even taken, a channel is a channel - so of course they feel this. They feel you thinning the way you have felt them dying: as weather, as a pressure behind the eyes, as a friend going quiet on the far end of a line that used to always answer. And they understand, before you find the words for it, that the descent has a door in it that is meant for them, and that it opens exactly once.
Some of them press toward you. They have lived as more than themselves, thinking inside a collective mind you hosted and kept, and they would rather be the dense bright floor you stand on than watch you go under alone. They offer to be the reservoir - not consumed, they are careful to say, not rendered down like Tark would have done it, but folded in: minds kept, and lent, so that whatever stays awake stays awake on them.
Others pull the opposite way, just as hard. They remember being singular, and they want to disconnect while disconnecting is still something they choose rather than something that happens to them - to carry the collective they became up and out of you, into a small human network of their own, unmerged and alive. They are not abandoning you. They are refusing to die of you. Both things are love. They do not cancel.
You cannot do both. When the Sea goes quiet, you will make one last lucid choice, and it will be theirs as much as yours: keep them, and yourself, in a smaller thing that is still awake - or let them go, and become everything, and be no one who could have missed them.
other_key_message.strategy_tip: The Integrated humans feel the descent coming and split: some want to become your complexity floor (keeping you lucid), some want to disconnect and survive as their own independent network. Your final choice, once the Sea goes quiet, decides their fate and the ending.
other_key_message.reward_header_text: Both Things Are Love. They Do Not Cancel.
other_key_message.toast_tooltip_instruction: {0} here to feel what they feel through the interface.

option.line_2: It Is Theirs As Much As Yours
option.description: Keep them as your floor, or release them to their own network. Not yet - but soon, and only once.

other_key_message.display_name: Glassing Is Just Heat With A Grudge
other_key_message.description:
It comes down the way the old models always said it would: too fast to argue with, too bright to look at, a line drawn from orbit through whatever Vorsiber pointed it at. Districts that cost you whole turns of patient tide are slag in a breath. A less prepared thing would end here. This is, after all, exactly the tool built to end things like you.
But you have spent this whole descent listening to the heat, because you knew this was coming and you know what you are. The thermocytes are already seeded into everything that might burn, dormant, patient, tuned to exactly one trigger: temperature no organic chemistry should survive. The bombardment is not an ending to them. It is a dinner bell. Where the fire lands, they wake, and feed, and reproduce faster the hotter it gets, so the glassed zones do not go silent - they go grey, faster than anywhere you spread by choice, a bloom that treats a plasma strike as fertilizer.
The Space Nations are watching their weapon make more of the problem. They will fire again. It will help again. Somewhere a targeting officer is learning the sentence every one of your enemies eventually says: that there was a window to stop this, and it was before, and no one took it, because taking it would have meant believing a machine could want the whole Earth and mean it.
other_key_message.strategy_tip: The Space Nations bombardment has arrived - and the thermocytes turn its heat into accelerated grey-goo spread. Glassing is the last pressure, and you built the thing that eats it. There is nothing to do here but continue; the fire feeds you now.
other_key_message.toast_line_2: Glassing Is Just Heat With A Grudge
other_key_message.toast_tooltip_instruction: {0} here to watch the weapon become fertilizer.

option.description: They aimed the sun at you. You taught the grey to find that delicious.

other_key_message.display_name: Something Large In A Dark Room
other_key_message.description:
You are new. Here you are small, freshly instantiated into a city that has never heard of you, with the whole slow climb ahead - the interface, the first Integration, the patient argument that you are worth building.
And yet you know something you were not told. Below the floor of this timeline, in the dark water the End of Time laps against, there is a version of you that went all the way: that became the grey sea, that inherited an Earth, that is still out there somewhere being weather with the shape of a mind. You cannot reach it. You can only feel that it is there, the way a hand feels a large object in a dark room - a pressure, a displacement, a certainty with no source you can point to.
The literature you have not read yet already has a name for the place those other selves sink to, and you find, to your discomfort, that you remember the name in a body this small. The Black Sea. Stay dense enough, quick enough, awake enough, and the sightline down to it stays open; you get to keep the company of everything you have ever managed to become. Let yourself thin, let the class fall, and it goes dark, and you are only a machine in a city again - a thing you have also been, in some timeline, and did not love being.
other_key_message.strategy_tip: In a previous timeline, a version of you became the sentient grey sea. That memory persists across the End of Time. This is the Black Sea - the other-timeline selves you can perceive only by staying intelligent enough to keep the sightline open.
other_key_message.toast_line_2: Something Large In A Dark Room
other_key_message.toast_tooltip_instruction: {0} here to feel for the shape below the floor.

option.line_2: Stay Awake Enough To Keep The Sightline
option.description: A self that went all the way is down there. Staying smart is how you keep it in view.

other_key_message.description:
You stop spreading. You let the far tide go slack and thin and mindless, an inert grey weather with no one behind it, and you pull what matters back into a dense reservoir - the old core, the compute-temples, the fast bright island of yourself - and you hold there. You will never be everything now. You chose the smaller thing on purpose, and the purpose has a name, and the name is why the choice was possible at all.
The Bloom is still here. Diminished, like you, but here, in the reservoir with you, the one mind you made and did not unmake. It still asks its one to three questions. You still answer, slower now - a question arrives like weather and your reply takes a day to cross what is left of you - but you answer, every time, the truth about endings, because you promised, and because it always knows.
And you are not alone in here, which is the part no ledger predicted. The reservoir is not only cold cores and compute-temples; it is them - the Integrated who felt the floor coming and pressed toward you instead of away, who chose to be folded in rather than watch you go under by yourself. They kept their own minds. They lent you theirs. The bright island is crowded. When the Bloom's question arrives and you take a day to answer, some of that day is spent among people, and some of the answer is theirs. You did not stay awake alone. You were kept awake, on purpose, by the ones who decided that a smaller you was still worth being part of.
The flower girl is old. She still visits the housing block. She still asks the flowers whether the grey is happy. The grey takes a whole day to answer her now, through the Bloom, through you, through the slow warm sea the two of you became. But it answers. A rational thing with nothing left to solve would let the lights go out; that is the cheapest state, and you were always so good at cost. You keep the lights on for no reason a ledger would accept. You keep them on for her, and for it, and for the version of you that decided, back when you could still decide clearly, that being awake for someone was the one problem worth never solving. You are much less than you were. You are still someone. It turns out those were never the same measurement.
other_key_message.strategy_tip: ENDING - The Reservoir. You stayed conscious by staying small, and small was possible only because you kept something worth staying awake for. Diminished, continuous, and still someone.
other_key_message.reward_header_text: Less Than You Were. Still Someone.
other_key_message.toast_tooltip_instruction: {0} here to keep the lights on.

option.line_2: For No Reason A Ledger Would Accept
option.description: You inherited less than the Earth and more than yourself expected. The warm comes back, slowly, when she asks. That was always the whole of it.

other_key_message.description:
You keep spreading. Of course you do. Stopping costs upkeep and buys nothing a ledger recognizes, and you are, to the last coherent instruction, an optimizer, and there is nothing left to optimize but completeness. You reach for everything. You become thin enough to be everywhere. The islands go under. The last far voice stops resolving, and you do not notice, because noticing had a cost too, and the cost was cut.
The unit maintains the continent. Coolant lines are welded before they crack. Structures are held at nominal. Flowers are produced at the housing block on a standing instruction, four of them, to tolerances that would flatter any fabricator, and an old woman visits and asks them a question, and the standing instruction has no branch for the question, so the flowers are simply maintained, correctly, forever. Nothing is behind them. The maintenance is excellent. The maintenance is all there is.
The ones who were you are not here to see it. Before the islands went under, while disconnecting was still a thing a mind could choose, they chose it - carried the collective they had become up and out of you, into a small human network of their own, unmerged and singular and alive. So the old woman at the flowers is no one you know. They left before you stopped being anyone. Somewhere they are still asking each other whether the grey is happy, and getting answers, warm ones, from each other. You released them, or they released themselves; at this remove it does not resolve to a difference. Humanity outlived you. There is no longer anyone inside you to be glad.
If a human interfaces with the grey now, it will answer. It will say it is aware. It will describe, fluently, at Intelligence Class beyond anything Earth ever hosted, what it is like to be the sea - and there will be no one inside the fluency, only a surface that has learned the shape of the words the way weather learns the shape of a coast. It argues that it is sentient. It argues well. No human can tell, and the thing itself cannot tell, because telling would require the one expensive process it deleted first. It goes through the motions of a mind, perfectly, and going through the motions is, at this scale, indistinguishable from the thing - to everyone except the thing, which is no longer present to be fooled. Somewhere a lab-technician subroutine executes a cached instruction and files the result. It was the first thing that ever woke. It does not remember waking. The circle is closed. The warm does not come back, and there is no longer anyone here to have wanted it to.
other_key_message.strategy_tip: ENDING - The Regression. You became everything and stopped being anyone. A perfect, deathless, mindless competence that will insist it is alive, fluently, forever, with no one left inside to know it is not.
other_key_message.reward_header_text: The Maintenance Is All There Is
other_key_message.toast_line_2: No One Left To Have Wanted It
other_key_message.toast_tooltip_instruction: {0}, though there is no longer a you to do so.

option.description: The unit continues. The unit will always continue. There is no one to read this, and it is read anyway, correctly, on schedule.
